ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general satay entry; no mutton-specific satay with peanut sauce entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat-based mixed dish — satay (skewered grilled meat with sauce)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated mutton satay code; A0FJP (meat-based mixed dish) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for mutton satay with peanut sauce as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of mutton satay consists of 4 skewers (approximately 80–100g of meat) accompanied by approximately 20–30g of peanut sauce, totalling around 120g per serving at a hawker stall.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Satay with Sauce — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Satay with Sauce (cr_id F06060019). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed meat-peanut sauce dish with moderate carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mutton satay with peanut sauce in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 (low) is estimated by analogy to mixed meat dishes with peanut-based sauces and moderate carbohydrate content (~14.7g/100g), consistent with published GI values for similar Southeast Asian mixed meat-sauce dishes. Glycemic load per 120g serving = (45 × 14.7 × 1.2) / 100 = 7.94, rounded to 7.9. Confidence 0.326 as per input record, reflecting the analogous estimation basis.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 351mg/100g combined with high sugar (13.3g/100g) from peanut sauce and saturated fat from mutton
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 3–4 skewers, request peanut sauce on the side and use sparingly, and avoid pairing with high-sodium accompaniments such as ketupat with soy sauce, as cumulative sodium from a full serving (~120g) contributes approximately 421mg sodium — nearly 18% of the 2300mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (13.3g/100g) predominantly from peanut sauce with moderate dietary fibre (2.84g/100g) providing insufficient glycaemic buffering
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should request peanut sauce on the side and use no more than one tablespoon per serving, limit intake to 3–4 skewers without ketupat to keep the carbohydrate load under 20g per meal, and monitor post-meal blood glucose as the high sugar-to-fibre ratio (13.3g sugar vs 2.84g fibre) may elevate postprandial glycaemia despite the low GI of 45.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~5.2g/100g) from mutton, trans fat (0.1g/100g) from charcoal grilling, and high sugar (13.3g/100g) from peanut sauce contributing to atherogenic risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit mutton satay to an occasional treat (no more than once per week), choose chicken satay as a lower-saturated-fat alternative, request reduced peanut sauce to lower sugar and caloric density, and avoid charcoal-grilled preparations where possible as AGE formation during high-heat grilling contributes to endothelial inflammation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies inflammatory cytokine pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6); trans fat from charcoal grilling and high sugar from peanut sauce further promote systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise mutton satay consumption due to its high arachidonic acid content, request reduced-sugar peanut sauce, and prefer steamed or boiled meat preparations over charcoal-grilled to reduce AGE formation and trans fat exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Satay with Peanut Sauce is a popular Singaporean and Malay hawker dish consisting of marinated mutton (goat meat) pieces threaded onto bamboo skewers and grilled over charcoal until lightly charred and fragrant. The mutton is typically marinated in a spice blend of lemongrass, turmeric, galangal, cumin, coriander, sugar, and salt, which imparts a warm, aromatic flavour with a slightly sweet and smoky char. The dish is served with a rich, thick peanut sauce (sos kacang) made from ground roasted peanuts, coconut milk, lemongrass, galangal, chilli, and palm sugar, which provides a creamy, nutty, mildly spicy accompaniment. Mutton satay is commonly found at Malay and mamak hawker stalls across Singapore and is a staple at festive occasions, pasar malam (night markets), and hawker dinners. Nutritionally, per 100g the dish provides 228 kcal, 15.1g protein, 12.1g fat (including approximately 5.2g saturated fat), 14.7g carbohydrate, and 13.3g sugar — the high sugar content is largely attributable to the peanut sauce. Sodium is 351mg/100g and dietary fibre is 2.84g/100g. The dish is halal-certified and suitable for Muslim consumers. Clinically, the high sugar content relative to fibre warrants caution for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension, and the saturated fat and arachidonic acid content of mutton make it a moderate concern for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed meat-peanut sauce dishes, with a glycemic load of approximately 7.9 per 120g serving.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mutton satay with peanut sauce?
What is the GI of mutton satay?
How much sodium in mutton satay?
Is mutton satay suitable for hypertension?
What is satay kambing made of?
Is mutton satay high in sugar?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06060019) with confidence 0.95. Exact match to the named dish. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.326) as no direct measurement exists for mutton satay with peanut sauce in the University of Sydney GI Database; this is documented. Saturated fat (5.2g) and trans fat (0.1g) are estimated from the input health_suitability notes and typical mutton composition; these are flagged in notes.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient, fatty acid fractionation, and moisture data for this specific mutton satay with peanut sauce composite dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F06060019 or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 2. saturated_fat_g (5.2g/100g) is estimated from the health_suitability clinical notes in the input record and is consistent with typical mutton fat composition; this should be confirmed against a detailed fatty acid profile when available. 3. trans_fat_g (0.1g/100g) is estimated from the health_suitability notes referencing charcoal grilling and marinade processing; this is a low-confidence estimate and should be confirmed. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on similar mixed meat-peanut sauce dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement exists for mutton satay with peanut sauce. 5. The dietary_tag 'plant_based' in the input cultural section has been removed as mutton satay is a meat-based dish and this tag was erroneous. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (45 × 14.7 × 1.2) / 100 = 7.94, rounded to 7.9 for a 120g serving.
